properties.name: Westridge Marine Terminal
properties.inputs: Trans Mountain Pipeline System (light crude + diluted bitumen from Edmonton)
properties.exportMarkets: Asia (China, India, South Korea), US West Coast refineries
properties.vesselTypes: Aframax tankers (~750,000 bbl per cargo) — the largest class the Burrard Inlet can accommodate
keyFacts: The only Canadian crude oil export terminal on the Pacific Coast — strategic Asian-market access.
TMX expansion (June 2024) tripled effective Westridge throughput from ~300K to ~890K bbl/d, materially shifting Canadian crude trade flows.
Aframax-only — the dock and vessel size are constrained by the geography of Burrard Inlet (Second Narrows depth + width).
Canadian crude (mostly diluted bitumen) leaving Westridge has narrowed the WCS / WTI price differential by reaching new buyers post-2024.
Owned by the Government of Canada (via Trans Mountain Corp.) since the 2018 federal acquisition of Kinder Morgan Canada.
properties.notes: On the map: pin sits at the Burrard Inlet anchorage; visually adjacent to where the Trans Mountain pipeline terminus pin would be.

properties.name: LNG Canada (Kitimat)
properties.operator: LNG Canada JV (Shell · Petronas · Mitsubishi · KOGAS · PetroChina) — Shell-operated
properties.volume: Phase 1: 14 Mtpa LNG (~1.8 Bcf/d feedgas equivalent); Phase 2 expansion would double
properties.inputs: Coastal GasLink pipeline (Montney basin gas via Dawson Creek)
properties.exportMarkets: Asia-Pacific (Japan, South Korea, China, Taiwan) — primary buyers via long-term offtake contracts
properties.vesselTypes: Q-Flex / Q-Max LNG carriers (~210,000 m³ to ~265,000 m³ per cargo)
keyFacts: First major Canadian LNG export terminal — first cargoes loading in mid-2025 after a 7-year construction.
$40B+ CAD capital project — one of the largest single private-sector investments in Canadian history.
Shell holds 40% and is the operator; Petronas, Mitsubishi, KOGAS, and PetroChina hold the remaining 60%.
Phase 1 capacity is 14 Mtpa across two trains; FID for Phase 2 (doubling capacity) pending.
Marks the start of Canada's commercial LNG era — historically Canada had built no LNG export terminals despite decades of debate.
properties.notes: Kitimat sits at the head of Douglas Channel — a deepwater fjord connecting to the Pacific via Hecate Strait. Tankers transit the channel under Coast Guard escort.

properties.name: Saint John Tanker Terminal
properties.volume: Two-way: ~300K bbl/d crude imports + ~150K bbl/d refined product exports
properties.inputs: Imported crude (Saudi Arabia, US Gulf, North Sea — diversified post-2014); no Canadian pipeline access
properties.exportMarkets: US East Coast (gasoline, diesel via tanker to PADD 1) — about half of Irving Saint John refinery output is exported
properties.vesselTypes: VLCC and Suezmax crude tankers inbound; Medium-Range product tankers outbound
keyFacts: The largest oil port on the Canadian East Coast — supplies the Irving Saint John refinery (Canada's largest, ~320K bbl/d).
No pipeline access from WCSB — all crude inputs arrive by tanker (rail volumes have come and gone with arbitrage economics).
Adjacent to the (idle) Canaport LNG import terminal, which was repurposed-for-export plans were paused after the 2014 oil-price crash.
Tidewater location enables both Saudi-Mideast and US Gulf crude inputs and US East Coast product exports.
Owned by the Irving family (Irving Oil + J.D. Irving Limited) — one of Canada's largest privately-held conglomerates.
properties.notes: Tanker dock is at Mispec Point, ~5 km east of the Saint John refinery itself; connected by Irving's own pipeline.

properties.name: Cedar LNG
properties.operator: Cedar LNG Project Ltd. (Haisla Nation 50.1% · Pembina Pipeline 49.9%)
properties.volume: Planned: 3 Mtpa LNG (target service date late 2028)
properties.inputs: Coastal GasLink pipeline (Montney basin gas) — same upstream feed as LNG Canada
properties.exportMarkets: Asia-Pacific — long-term offtake contracts being finalized; smaller scale than LNG Canada targets premium spot pricing
properties.vesselTypes: Mid-scale LNG carriers (~125,000 m³ per cargo) — smaller than LNG Canada's Q-Flex fleet
keyFacts: First Indigenous-majority-owned LNG export project in the world — Haisla Nation holds majority (50.1%) ownership.
FID (final investment decision) reached in June 2023; estimated ~$3.4B CAD capital cost.
Floating LNG (FLNG) facility — barge-mounted liquefaction units rather than land-based trains, which speeds construction.
Pembina Pipeline Corp. is the technical operator (49.9% partner); Haisla holds majority equity and ultimate decision-making.
Often paired with LNG Canada in 'Canadian Pacific LNG' narrative — together they anchor Kitimat as Canada's gas-export hub.
properties.notes: Site is at Bish Cove, ~25 km south of LNG Canada at Kitimat — same Douglas Channel marine corridor.

properties.name: Woodfibre LNG
properties.volume: Planned: 2.1 Mtpa LNG (target first cargo late 2027)
properties.inputs: FortisBC's Eagle Mountain–Woodfibre Gas Pipeline (FortisBC system, BC-sourced gas)
properties.exportMarkets: Asia-Pacific — primarily Japan and South Korea via long-term BP offtake contract
properties.vesselTypes: Mid-scale LNG carriers (~165,000 m³ per cargo)
keyFacts: Smallest of the three under-construction Canadian LNG projects — a 'right-sized' play vs LNG Canada's mega-scale.
Owned by Pacific Energy (ultimately RGE Group, a Singaporean conglomerate); BP holds the long-term offtake.
Located on the site of a former pulp mill in Squamish — uses existing brownfield infrastructure to speed permitting.
FID reached in 2022 after years of regulatory and Indigenous-consent process; first electric-drive LNG plant in North America.
Howe Sound location places tankers within ~50 km of Vancouver — controversial for marine traffic + viewscape concerns.
properties.notes: Squamish sits at the head of Howe Sound; tankers transit via the Sound to Strait of Georgia and out the Juan de Fuca Strait.
